Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of all over the place this semester. I'd plan to study around 33 hours a week, and I'd block out time in my calendar, but then I'd get anxious about everything piling up and end up just watching lecture videos without really absorbing much. I'm a visual learner, so I tried to focus on diagrams and video content, but my mind would wander a lot. I'd often study late at night when my anxiety was worst, which probably wasn't the smartest approach.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. Early in the semester I tried to stick to a regular schedule, but as things got more overwhelming, I'd cram before deadlines and then barely touch anything for days after. My extracurriculars also took up time, and sometimes I'd prioritize those just to feel like I was accomplishing something. The inconsistency really hurt me in the end.
Q3: Usually it was anxiety that prompted me to log in, honestly. I'd wake up worried that I'd missed something important or that a deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements, even if I wasn't planning to study. Other times it was because I got a notification about a discussion post or assignment. The platform became this source of stress where I felt like I needed to monitor it constantly.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS in the morning became automatic, like checking social media. I'd grab my coffee and scroll through the course page to see what was new. It felt routine during the first month or so, but then as my grades started slipping and my anxiety increased, logging in started feeling more like a chore. The routine broke down when I started dreading what I might find there.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I watched almost 280 minutes of video content because seeing concepts explained helped more than reading. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful too when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. I also appreciated when instructors posted visual summaries or diagrams alongside the regular materials.
Q6: I often ignored the reading-heavy resources and some of the supplementary PDFs. When I'm already anxious, staring at walls of text just makes me zone out completely. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but rarely did. Some of the optional practice problems I also skipped because I was so focused on just getting through the required stuff that I couldn't justify spending time on extras.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to an assignment or exam topic. Visual content that broke down complex ideas made me feel like I was actually learning something. If something took too long without clear payoff, my motivation would tank and I'd start questioning why I was even bothering. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped sometimes when I could focus enough to do them.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford mentally. Required ones came with this weight of obligation and fear of failing, which paradoxically made them harder to start. With optional stuff, there was no external pressure, so I'd skip them when stressed. But sometimes the optional content was actually more interesting because there was no grade attached, so I could engage without the anxiety of being evaluated.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials and studied from them offline. I'd take screenshots of important diagrams and review them on my phone when I was commuting or waiting around. I also used some YouTube videos and external resources that explained concepts differently than the course materials. My notes app is full of stuff I typed up while studying that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with my printed notes or downloaded PDFs, going over concepts for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd sometimes watch a lecture video once online, then replay it from a downloaded copy multiple times. Study groups with friends happened completely outside the platform too. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a video or document once to download it, then work with it offline for days. My attendance in the LMS looks terrible at around 20%, but that doesn't account for all the times I was reviewing downloaded content. I preferred working offline sometimes because it reduced the temptation to check other notifications and helped with my anxiety about being constantly connected.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. My anxiety spiked to the point where I'd open the LMS, see all the pending work, and just freeze. There were days I couldn't bring myself to engage at all. The stress was at a medium level consistently, but my ability to cope was low. Personal stuff was also happening, and my resilience just wasn't there to push through like I normally would.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and other courses, the LMS became something I avoided. Confusion about certain topics made me procrastinate because I didn't know where to start. Sometimes I'd log in, feel the cognitive overload hit me, and just log right back out without doing anything productive. My task completion rate suffered because of this cycle.
Q14: Many times. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Sometimes I'd open an assignment, stare at it for twenty minutes, and close it without writing anything. The engagement looked real on paper but mentally I was checked out. It's frustrating because I wanted to engage but couldn't.
Q15: I postponed studying for the exam way too long, even though I knew it was the most important thing. The anxiety made it hard to start because I was afraid of confirming how behind I was. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day rarely came. The assignments I did complete, I actually scored well on, but the ones I avoided just kept piling up until it was too late.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming everything in at the last minute. The LMS probably shows these spikes clearly. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate. But the deadline-driven approach meant I never really learned deeply, just enough to submit something.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me briefly. Seeing that I scored 82 or 83 on assignments made me feel capable, which helped my mood temporarily. But when feedback was delayed or when I did poorly on something, it reinforced my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course altogether. The inconsistency of my emotional response to feedback made it hard to build momentum.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming at times. There was a lot of content, and I struggled to prioritize what mattered most. The two online courses I was taking simultaneously made it hard to keep track of everything. When the structure was clear and modular, I did better. When it felt like everything was interconnected and I'd missed foundational stuff, I'd spiral into avoidance.
Q19: Having video content made engagement easier for me as a visual learner. But the sheer volume of materials made it harder. When there were too many resources for a single topic, I'd get paralyzed trying to decide what to focus on. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me manage my anxiety and time better.
Q20: That sounds like me in some ways. I logged in frequently and watched a lot of videos, but my assignment completion was only around 57%. I think sometimes you're trying to engage, you're present on the platform, but you can't translate that into productive output. Anxiety, confusion, or just not knowing how to start can create this gap between activity and actual submitted work.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies mostly offline or has strong foundational knowledge, they might not need to use the LMS much but still do well. For me, it was kind of the opposite. I was active on the LMS but my exam score was low because the engagement wasn't translating into real understanding. Activity doesn't equal learning, I've learned that the hard way.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, how long you spend on videos, and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're anxious, distracted, or actually absorbing information. They also miss all the offline studying, the mental processing that happens away from the screen, and the emotional state that affects everything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling mentally present when you're studying. It's not just logging in or watching videos, it's understanding and connecting with the material. For me, real engagement also means managing my anxiety enough to actually focus. This semester, I was often present but not truly engaged because my mind was elsewhere.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally and mentally throughout this course. It wouldn't capture the panic attacks before logging in, the hours spent worrying instead of studying, or the effort it took just to open an assignment. It also wouldn't show that I actually understood some concepts well when I could focus, or that I genuinely wanted to succeed but couldn't overcome the barriers in my head.
